data.title: Thermo Fisher: Great Business Still Trading At A Risky Price Before Earnings
data.description:
In July 2025, I published my first and only article about American life science and clinical research company Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (TMO). I rated the stock as a “Hold” and wrote in my conclusion:

Thermo Fisher is certainly a great business with a wide economic moat around its business. However, I see the stock as fairly valued at best, as I don’t see the company growing in the double digits in the years to come. In the last ten years, the acquisition contributed to growth but due to rather high debt levels and very high amounts of goodwill on the balance sheet, I don’t know if Thermo Fisher can continue this strategy. Therefore, I rather see growth rates in the high single digits being reasonable and the stock will remain on my watchlist – together with Waters Corp and Danaher Corporation.

Despite my “Hold” rating and rather cautious stance, the stock increased slightly above 20% in value and clearly outperformed the S&P 500. In the meantime, the stock increased even 50% to $643 and came close to its all-time high once again before the correction drove the stock down again (in line with many other stocks around the world).

In the following article, I will look at Thermo Fisher again before the company will report its first quarter results on April 23, 2026. And while we can argue that Thermo Fisher will perform stably in the coming quarters and economic uncertainty probably won’t have a huge impact on the business, I still consider the stock price too expensive at this point.

Results Fiscal 2025

Let’s start by looking at the full-year results. Total revenue increased from $42,879 million in fiscal 2024 to $44,556 million in fiscal 2025, resulting in 3.9% year-over-year growth for the top line. Operating income increased 5.6% year-over-year from $7,337 million in the previous year to $7,746 million in fiscal 2025. Diluted earnings per share also increased from $16.53 in fiscal 2024 to $17.74 in fiscal 2025, resulting in 7.3% year-over-year bottom line growth. Only free cash flow declined 13.5% year-over-year from $7,324 million in fiscal 2024 to $6,337 million in fiscal 2025.

When looking at the fourth quarter results, we see even better results. Revenue increased from $11,395 million in Q4/24 to $12,215 million in Q4/25, resulting in 7.2% year-over-year growth for the top line. Operating income increased 11.9% year-over-year from $2,016 million in the same quarter last year to $2,255 million in this quarter. Finally, diluted earnings per share increased from $4.78 in Q4/24 to $5.21 in Q4/25, resulting in 9.0% year-over-year growth.

Growth: Continuing Solid Pace

Management remains optimistic for fiscal 2026 and issued an optimistic guidance in January 2026. During the last earnings call, management commented:

Turning now to guidance. As Marc outlined, we're initiating a 2026 revenue guidance range of $46.3 billion to $47.2 billion and an adjusted EPS guidance range of $24.22 to $24.80, representing 6% to 8% growth.

The guidance assumes 3% to 4% organic revenue growth, a $300 million revenue tailwind from foreign exchange and 50 basis points of adjusted operating margin expansion. All of this will enable a really strong 6% to 8% growth in adjusted EPS. This guidance is consistent with the financial framing for '26 and '27 that we shared with you back on our Q2 earnings call. It reflects a continued improvement in our organic growth in '26, coupled with very strong earnings growth. The strength of our guidance reflects our industry-leading position, our proven growth strategy and the power of our PPI Business System.

And if the Clario acquisition is closed in mid-2026, expected earnings per share will be about $0.25. During the earnings call, management commented:

The guide, therefore, does not include the benefit of the pending acquisition of Clario.

As a reminder, we expect this deal to close by the middle of 2026. Should that be the case, we would expect $0.20 to $0.25 of incremental adjusted EPS for this year, reflecting the strongly accretive nature of the acquisition.

This would result in revenue growing about 4% to 6% on a reported basis and organic growth being in a range of 3% to 4%. Adjusted earnings per share are expected to grow between 6% and 8.5% (when including the acquisition, growth rates will be between 7% and 9.5%).

Analysts are also expecting earnings per share to grow at a solid pace in the years to come. Analysts are expecting earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 9.4% between fiscal 2025 and fiscal 2029.

Academic Funding Cuts

In my last article, I already mentioned the problem that the budget cuts by the Trump administration for research and academic institutions will have a negative impact on Thermo Fisher’s business. Especially the end market, Academic & Government, might be affected here, and in fiscal 2025, it was responsible for 14% of total revenue (after 15% in fiscal 2024), and revenue declined in the low single digits in the fourth quarter and full year 2025—mostly due to macroeconomic conditions in the United States and China.

During the last earnings call, management was slightly optimistic that the budget won’t be cut further:

And when I think about the U.S. environment, our assumption here is that there'll be a level of customer caution that will probably abate as the year goes down. But I would still assume in our guidance, it will be a more cautious environment as customers are navigating the landscape. It seems likely that we'll get a flat to slightly up NIH budget. That's going to be a good point in time. Exactly when that happens, TBD. So that should create tailwinds. When I think about over the next couple of years, I would expect that, that will be one of the drivers of us higher in the range. But for now, our assumption is that relatively cautious for 2026.

Unstable World

When looking at the chart above, we also see that the biggest part of revenue is stemming from pharmaceuticals and biotech, and it will be especially important whether these companies will continue spending or maybe cut capital expenditures. And of course, we also must keep in mind that 84% of revenue is stemming from services and consumables. Hence, it is not the decisive question whether companies and academia will buy new instruments, but whether they are continuing to use existing instruments in a similar way as before. And usually, we are not really talking about high levels of cyclicality. Academic research or the R&D spending of pharmaceutical companies is not as recession-sensitive as many other businesses.

Despite the business being rather stable, we should not ignore that the world is very unstable at this point, and the high oil price and high levels of uncertainty might have a huge negative impact on the economy and many businesses in the United States (and other countries around the world). And of course, Thermo Fisher is dependent on the capital expenditures and spending of its customers, who might cut back a little in uncertain times.

On the other hand, especially services and consumables, are essential for pharmaceutical businesses, and it is difficult to reduce spending, as research is extremely important to bring new products to market and generate revenue. Therefore, it seems unlikely for Thermo Fisher to see its revenue decline due to the war between the United States and Iran or higher oil prices.

Growth Strategy

Aside from macroeconomic trends and events, Thermo Fisher continues its growth strategy that consists of 3 pillars: high-impact innovation, the company’s trusted partner status with customers, and the company’s unparalleled commercial engine. And the company will try to grow by using a mix of acquisitions and share buybacks (and it is also paying a dividend):

Let me now turn to capital deployment. We continue to successfully execute our proven capital deployment strategy, which is a combination of strategic M&A and returning capital to shareholders. 2025 was a very active year as we advanced our strategy and added exciting new capabilities that further strengthen our long-term competitive position and create value for all of our stakeholders. During the year, we deployed approximately $16.5 billion, including committing $13 billion to M&A and returning $3.6 billion to shareholders through stock buybacks and dividends.

However, share buybacks contributed only a little bit to bottom-line growth in the past. Since 2018, the company decreased the number of diluted shares outstanding with a CAGR of 0.9%.

While share buybacks contributed only a little bit to bottom-line growth, the constantly improving operating margin contributed a lot to earnings per share growth during the last two decades.

And in the recent past, the company was closing several acquisitions. This is including the completed acquisition of the filtration and separation business from Solventum as well as the acquisition of Clario, which will probably close in 2026. During the last earnings call, management commented:

During the fourth quarter, we also announced a definitive agreement to acquire Clario. The company is a market leader in digital endpoint data solutions, one of the fastest-growing areas and an essential capability in drug development and clinical research. In 2025, the business generated approximately $1.25 billion in revenue. Its differentiated technology and deep medical expertise together enable unique capabilities in generating and delivering digital endpoint data for clinical trials.

And finally, the company expanded the U.S. drug product manufacturing footprint through the acquisition of Sanofi’s fill-finish site in New Jersey.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

One final problem remains—the high valuation multiples Thermo Fisher is trading for. At the time of writing, the stock is trading for 29 times earnings and for 31 times free cash flow. We certainly can make the argument that the stock has been trading for similar valuation multiples in the last ten years, and the stock is more or less in line with the 10-year average P/E and P/FCF ratio.

The problem with simple valuation multiples is always the same: they do not reflect growth rates in any way, and if Thermo Fisher can grow only in the mid-single digits in the years to come, a valuation multiple of 30 is probably too high. However, if the company can grow its bottom line in the double digits (like it has in the past), a valuation multiple of 30 could be justified.

Therefore, we are once again using a discounted cash flow calculation to determine an intrinsic value for the stock (which also enables us to calculate a few different scenarios with different assumptions). For every calculation, we are using a discount rate of 10% (the annual return we like to achieve for every investment) and using 377 million diluted shares outstanding (the last reported number).

As a basis for our calculation, we can use the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was $6,293 million. The company reported a free cash flow margin of 14.1%, which is slightly above the long-term average but seems like a reasonable basis we can use and more or less the margin that was reported in the last 10 years.

Now, we must determine a reasonable growth rate for the years to come. Using a rather cautious assumption, let’s assume 9% growth in the next ten years (in line with analysts’ assumptions) followed by 4% growth till perpetuity (according to CFA recommendations for terminal growth rates). This leads to an intrinsic value of $387.96, and the stock remains clearly overvalued at this point.

We can also make the argument that Thermo Fisher is a high-quality business, and for such a company, we can also calculate with 6% growth till perpetuity.

When calculating with these assumptions, we get an intrinsic value of $516.09 for Thermo Fisher, and the stock would be fairly valued. And when looking at previous growth rates, we can also assume double-digit growth rates for the next ten years. In the last ten years, Thermo Fisher grew its bottom line with a CAGR of 13.68%, and in the years before, the 10-year CAGR was even around 20%.

Upcoming Results

On April 23, 2026, Thermo Fisher will report its first quarter results for fiscal 2026, and revenue is expected to be $10.86 billion. Compared to a revenue of $10.36 billion in Q1/25, this would result in about 4.8% growth. Earnings per share are expected to be $3.90 on a GAAP basis, and compared to $3.99 in Q1/25, this would result in a 2.3% year-over-year decline.

It is also worth mentioning that all analysts (17 in total) lowered estimates for the upcoming quarter, and over the last two years, we have rather seen estimates being lowered over time. Considering the lower estimates and the highly unstable economic situation, it seems like a reasonable assumption that Thermo Fisher might lower its guidance for fiscal 2026. However, I do not assume management will lower its guidance as the company is not so dependent on shipping rates or oil prices and has a business that is more or less recession resilient. Nevertheless, we should pay close attention to management’s statements during the earnings call and whether management’s statements are still optimistic or are getting rather cautious.

Conclusion

It seems understandable that investors want a high level of stability and predictability and would rather purchase stocks in companies like Thermo Fisher. But the current price is probably not justified for the stock, and the high price is making Thermo Fisher a rather mediocre investment. We are certainly dealing with a great, high-quality business, but the stock still seems to be a bit too expensive.

data.title: GH Research: WH Decision On Psychedelics To Treat Depression Makes Bull Case
data.description:
"n.b. this post was written prior to market open today - in early trading, the stock has risen further in value, to $24 at the time of writing, up >25%, Current market cap valuation has now risen to $1.47bn."

Investment Overview

The stock of Dublin, Ireland-based biotech GH Research PLC (GHRS) was surging last week, climbing nearly 20% in value, to $18.3 per share, its highest value since April 2022. The stock is now up >40% year-to-date and ~110% on a one-year basis.

GH Research discusses its business as follows in its 2025 annual report / 10-K submission:

We are a clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company dedicated to transforming the lives of patients by developing a practice-changing treatment in depression. Our initial focus is on developing our novel and proprietary mebufotenin therapies for the treatment of patients with treatment-resistant depression, or TRD.

Our portfolio currently includes GH001, our proprietary inhalable mebufotenin product candidate and GH002, our proprietary intravenous mebufotenin product candidate. While GH001 is currently delivered via a vaporization device produced by a third party, we are developing a proprietary aerosol delivery device, which is currently in clinical investigation in Europe.

Last week's gains were driven by the news that U.S. President Trump would be signing an executive order aimed at accelerating research and expanding access to substances used abroad to treat post-traumatic stress disorder.

According to a White House statement:

Policymakers and the medical field have long struggled to address the burden of suicide and serious mental illness rates in America. Today, over 14 million American adults have a serious mental illness, defined as having a diagnosable mental, behavioral, or emotional disorder that substantially interferes with a person’s life and ability to function, and about 8 million are on prescription medication for these conditions. Suicide rates tragically increased by 37 percent from 2000 to 2018.

Critically, veterans often suffer in greater measure from this tragedy. For over 20 years, there have been more than 6,000 veteran suicides per year, and the current veteran suicide rate is more than twice as much as the non-veteran adult population.

Despite massive Federal investment into researching potential advancements in mental health care and treatment, our medical research system has yet to produce approved therapies that promote enduring improvements in the mental health condition of these most complex patients.

Psychedelic drugs, including ibogaine compounds, show potential in clinical studies to address serious mental illnesses for patients whose conditions persist after completing standard therapy. Indeed, the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has granted Breakthrough Therapy designation to specific psychedelic drugs, and there are numerous products currently in the clinical trial pipeline for review of safety and efficacy.

When I last covered GH Research in March 2023, I assigned stock a Hold rating, writing that:

As a speculative, long-term buy and hold I don't dislike GH as an investment opportunity, although it could be several more years before this company can be considered a as a commercial prospect as opposed to an innovative research firm.

At that time, shares were worth $8.7 per share, and they have been somewhat volatile since, typically trading between $5 and $15. In January this year, shares spiked by >40% as the Food and Drug Administration ("FDA") lifted a clinical hold that had been placed on the investigational new drug ("IND") application for GH001, paving the way for the company to run a pivotal Phase 3 study of its candidate.

As such, two years on from my last note, and with stock now up >110% since it was published, this feels like a good time to update on the company and consider whether a rating upgrade is warranted.

GH Research In 2026 - Study Data Suggests Approval May Be In Sight

First, let's take a look at GH Research's product pipeline, as shared in a slide in a recent corporate presentation:

GH Research notes in its 10K filing:

Our lead mebufotenin product candidate, GH001, is formulated for administration via a proprietary inhalation approach. For GH001, we use synthetically developed, pharmaceutical grade mebufotenin free base, manufactured in accordance with current Good Manufacturing Practices, or cGMP, standards.

According to Wikipedia, Mebufotenin is

"found in a wide variety of plant species, and also is secreted by the glands of at least one toad species, the Colorado River toad. Slang terms include Five-methoxy, the power, and Toad venom."

In the U.S., it is currently classed as a Schedule I substance - according to GH Research:

The DEA regulates drug substances and chemical compounds as Schedule I, II, III, IV or V substances. Schedule I substances by definition have a high potential for abuse, have no currently “accepted medical use”.

For any product containing mebufotenin to be available for commercial marketing in the United States, mebufotenin must be rescheduled to Schedule II, III, IV or V, or the DEA must reschedule a specific dosage form or product containing mebufotenin to Schedule II, III, IV or V. Similar rescheduling would be required in the various states and jurisdictions through scheduling-related legislative or administrative action.

This classification has arguably been the biggest obstacle to the approval of psychedelic drugs, but the White House statement from last week notes as follows (and amongst other things):

The Commissioner of Food and Drugs shall provide Commissioner’s National Priority Vouchers to appropriate psychedelic drugs that have received a Breakthrough Therapy designation and are in accordance with the criteria of the National Priority Voucher Program.

The Attorney General shall, in consultation with HHS, initiate and complete review of any product containing a Schedule I substance that has successfully completed Phase 3 clinical trials for a serious mental health disorder, so that rescheduling, if appropriate under 21 U.S.C. 811, may proceed as quickly as practicable for such specific products that are ultimately approved under section 505 of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act.

GH Research has presented the full data set from its Phase 2b study of GH001 at the 2025 American Society of Clinical Psychopharmacology (ASCP) Annual Meeting in Arizona and the 2025 European College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ECNP) Congress in Amsterdam - according to its 2025 earnings press release:

The Phase 2b trial met its primary endpoint with a placebo-adjusted Montgomery-Åsberg Depression Rating Scale (MADRS) reduction of -15.5 points at Day 8 (p<0.0001). In the double-blind portion, 57.5% of patients on GH001 achieved remission (MADRS ≤10) at Day 8 versus 0% on placebo.

The open-label extension confirmed durable efficacy, with a 73% remission rate at six months achieved with infrequent retreatment visits and no mandated psychotherapy. The median duration of psychoactive experience was ~11 minutes, with 99% of patients deemed discharge-ready within one hour of dosing. Safety was favorable, with no treatment-related serious adverse events and no treatment emergent suicidal intent or behavior.

The study enrolled patients with treatment-resistant depression ("TRD"), and apparently results were significantly superior to treatment with Spravato, an esketamine-derived drug first approved to treat TRD in 2019, in conjunction with an oral antidepressant, before securing approval as a monotherapy in January last year. Spravato, marketed and sold by the Pharma Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), earned ~$1.7bn last year.

As we can see, while it's never entirely fair to compare clinical studies, GH001 appears to have achieved a dramatically larger reduction in MADRS scoring than Spravato, while also achieving a superior rate of remission in patients (57.5% at 8 days, compared to ~10% for Spravato, GH Research says), with a 73% remission rate at six months, and defying the widely held view that patients' remission rates decline significantly with each successive antidepressant treatment failure.

Patients in the GH001 study also required an average of four treatment visits in 6 months, while Spravato patients complete ~23 visits over the same period (GH Research says, citing Institute for Clinical and Economic Review data). From a safety perspective, there were no treatment-related serious adverse events in the study and no treatment-emergent adverse events involving suicidal intent or behavior. In the open-label extension ("OLE") part of the study, GH Research notes:

MADRS reduction in the Placebo group following first active treatments after entering the OLE, was comparable to the results observed in the GH001 group in the DB part

As we can see in the slide above, GH Research is now confident that it has a "best-in-class" TRD therapeutic treatment, a best-in-class psychedelic, and a best-in-class molecule, and the company believes that, should GH001 be approved, it would qualify for five years' exclusivity in the U.S. (i.e., no generic versions for GH001 could be launched by competitors) and ten years in Europe if approved in that territory, while the product would also be patent protected, including its novel use as an inhalable product, its aerosol composition, and as a medical device.

Looking Ahead - Critical Phase 3 Study Dovetailing With Changing Attitudes

As of the end of 2025, GH Research reported a cash position of $281m, having completed a ~$150m funding round last year. R&D expenses for 2025 amounted to $38.8m, and G&A expenses were $22m. Net loss amounted to $(48.3m), or $(0.79) per share.

Discussing the clinical hold in its 2025 10-K filing, GH Research states:

in September 2023, our IND for GH001 was placed on a clinical hold by the FDA requiring an inhalation toxicology study in a non-rodent species and an additional inhalation toxicology study. We completed the toxicology studies and met with the FDA, and the clinical hold was lifted by the FDA in December 2025.

In summary, then, it seems as though the path to a full approval of GH001 is beginning to open up. The clinical hold has been lifted, and the U.S. government has expressed a willingness to consider fast-tracking approvals for psychedelic drugs and reclassifying them from Schedule I to make it easier to manufacture, transport, and distribute the therapy.

The equation therefore seems simple: succeed in its Phase 3 pivotal study, and with a Commissioner’s National Priority Voucher ("CNPV"), GH001 could be on the market a few months after its New Drug Application requesting commercial approval is submitted to the FDA (assuming it is accepted).

With its strong efficacy and safety profile and ability to restrict patient treatment visits, we may speculate that the therapy could take a significant amount of market share from existing standards of care, including Spravato, antipsychotic medicines Abilify (aripiprazole) and Rexulti (brexpiprazole), which earned a combined ~$1bn last year, and second-generation antipsychotic medications Seroquel (quetiapine) and Zyprexa (olanzapine).

GH Research's market cap valuation has risen above $1bn and currently stands at $1.14bn, which, in my view, may be a significant undervaluation of a company that may be one positive data set away from pushing for approval in a multi-billion dollar market, with a best-in-class safety and efficacy profile. I would speculate that GH001 could earn ~$500m - $1bn in peak annual revenues (perhaps more), and therefore, using a 3x forward price to peak sales ratio, I'd speculate GH Research merits a $1.5bn - $3bn market cap valuation today.

There are naturally risks to consider. For example, while the current government in the U.S. is taking steps to make it easier for psychedelic drugs to be approved to treat depression in the U.S., there is no guarantee that any legislation will be upheld by the next administration. Also, in terms of competition, GH Research notes:

Several biopharmaceutical companies have therapies, including other tryptamine compounds, in preclinical and clinical development being evaluated or planned to be evaluated in mental illness, including in TRD patients, including AtaiBeckley (ATAI), Axsome Therapeutics (AXSM), COMPASS Pathways (CMPS), Helus Pharma and Definium Therapeutics.

Of the programs with other tryptamine compounds, the most advanced is COMPASS Pathways’ investigational therapy COMP360 given in conjunction with psychological support, for which results of a Phase 2b trial in TRD have been reported and a Phase 3 program has been initiated.

Compass stock was also soaring last week, gaining ~13.5%, and the company has attained a market cap valuation of ~$640m, but its data seems somewhat less impressive than GH Research, in my view. Covering the company for Seeking Alpha in February this year (assigning a Hold rating – stock is down ~13% since), I noted its candidate COMP006, a psilocybin-based therapy, achieved a clinically meaningful difference of -3.8 points vs. placebo in MADRS scoring - data for GH0001 showed a >15 point reduction, as discussed above.

All things considered, then, and as noted above, the obstacles to GH001 securing approval in the U.S. in TRD appear to be being removed one by one - drug classification, clinical holds, lengthy review periods, positive data sets, funding, alignment with the FDA, and so on. Furthermore, approval in Europe may also be achievable, and there are also studies ongoing in postpartum depression and bipolar disorder, plus a second generation product, GH002 (mebufotenin for IV administration), now also in clinical studies.

While acknowledging some risks to the bull thesis – an unsuccessful pivotal study, numerous competitors, and the U.S. government not following up on its promise to fast-track approvals and reclassify drug products - I'd still make GH Research a solid Buy opportunity at this time, although given the recent gains in the share price and the long-ish wait for pivotal data (it took around two years for the Phase 2b study to complete and data to be shared, by my estimation), I'd also suggest there may be opportunities to buy stock at a cheaper price in the coming months.

Longer term, however, as discussed above, I'd speculate the true valuation of this company could be somewhere between $1.5bn and $3bn, given the exciting opportunity in play.

data.title: ICICI Bank: Solid Q4 Results Should Support Recovery From Recent Over-Correction
data.description:
ICICI Bank (IBN) is currently in the middle of a recovery after a sharp fall in March. Positive quarterly numbers, easing of the West Asia crisis, and some regulatory relief should give it some short-term tailwind that can be bought into for 10% or so short-term gain, even though the longer picture remains slightly unclear.

The 2025 Drift and March (Over)correction

ICICI Bank touched all-time high levels of just under $34 and stayed there for May, June, and July 2025, driven by a two-year-long golden era of peak net interest margins (NIM) with high-interest loans and low-interest deposits. The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) had a major rate-hike cycle from May 2022 to Feb 2023 (going from 4% to 6.5%). This made 2023 and 2024 a “sweet spot” where banks lent at repo but had to pay out fixed deposits on older rates. But eventually, the deposits had to be renewed at higher rates, and at the same time, the post-COVID hyper demand for credit started normalizing (loan growth went from 18-20% to 12-14%), while bank deposits faced stiff competition from mutual funds, who were grabbing an increasingly larger share of Indians’ savings—this led to an era of NIM compression, which dipped from a peak of 4.78% in Q1 FY2024 to 4.27% in Q2 FY2025. Analysts feared it might eventually revert to 4.0% levels, but it seemed to have stuck at those levels, ending Q3 FY2026 at 4.3%.

2025 also saw massive FII outflows from the Indian market due to rising US yields, with ICICI Bank being one of their main holdings. For ADR holders, the pain was increased by the poor performance of the Indian Rupee, causing it to drop sharply towards $25 in March. At the same time, the RBI increased capital requirements for banks on unsecured retail lending, creating an effective tax on ICICI’s most profitable segment. Then in early 2026, the RBI further tightened the regulatory noose and prohibited banks from lending to prop trading and required 100% collateral for lending to brokers and ending the automatic bundling of insurance with loans. The spiking West Asia conflict, coupled with regulatory tightening, led to massive bearish positions, with put activity hitting record highs. The usual put-call ratio of 0.8-1 spiked to 1.68 when the stock approached its 52-week low in the Indian markets, with record open interest levels.

The April Recovery and the Results

Since hitting bottom in March, the parent stock has staged a sharp 11% recovery in the Indian markets. The RBI firstly provided relief by deferring the capital requirement norms for brokers until July 2026. As the anxiety around the West Asia crisis started to ease, the open interest unwound with the put-call standing at an optimistic 0.52 (as of 4/19).

In between this relief rally, ICICI has posted solid Q4 2026 results, and the board has recommended an INR 12 dividend (a yield of around 0.64% for ADR holders after taxes). NIM has marginally ticked up to 4.32%, now solidifying this level as the new hopeful standard and seemingly avoiding the worst-case scenario of 4.0%. Y-o-Y, the bank has seen a loan growth of 15.8%. This is despite sluggish retail growth, with most of the new growth coming from Business Banking and Rural Portfolio (both contributing approx. 25%). While some caution lingers over the slowness of ICICI’s traditional stronghold of retail banking, this does demonstrate a healthy capacity to pivot—with their dominance in rural banking making the best use of the government’s mandatory guidelines to lend to priority sectors (mostly agriculture). This also introduced the monsoon outlook as a sentiment factor for the bank, which so far has been neutral.

A much talked about number is also the dramatic reduction in provisioning from INR 2,556 crore to INR 96 crore, a reduction of 96%. This was partly because of a one-time provision of INR 1,283 crore in the previous quarter due to an RBI mandate. But the rest is still very impressive, highlighting massive recoveries and upgrades (to the tune of INR 3,068 crore) backing ICICI’s reputation for a very healthy loan book, as well as the INR 13,100 crore contingency buffer that the bank is sitting on. This has also provided a near 3% boost to the bottom line, taking it to 8.5% even as the core operating profit grew at a more modest 5%.

Price Targets

The Q4 results aren’t transformative—but they are highly reassuring to what seems to be an oversold stock and are helping immensely in changing the narrative around it after it took a beating in March. A massive leadership shock and resulting uncertainty at one of its biggest rivals, HDFC Bank, also makes ICICI look good by comparison to returning investors. ICICI trades at 3x its book value, at a book value per share (BVPS) of INR 480 (calculated from the results). With 15.8% loan growth, the BVPS goes up to INR 550, which, at the 3x multiple, translates to a target price of INR 1,650, maintaining the 3x multiple. For the ICICI Bank ADR on the NYSE, this would translate to a price target of roughly $35, which is the peak level it had seen in mid-2025.

Now I’m not really the biggest fan of trying to “quant up” predictions based on quarterly results, but what I think is the important part here is that this is what the market is likely thinking right now. After a year-long drift and a steep crash in March, the narrative has changed to this being a stock that is, in theory, ready to go back to its relatively recent peak. This is a pretty strong momentum for a stock that has already bounced from $25 levels to $28 levels. What I’d really like to do here is to get in on the bounce right now and hold the stock until $32-33 levels for a 10-12% upside in the near term. The stock has not yet reacted to the results: if it goes up, ride the wave and get out quickly; if there is a bit of ‘sell the news,’ buy the dip and hold it for the quarter. Any further easing on the West Asia crisis should keep this propped up. After March, the downside also looks limited.

Risks and Conclusion

In the long term, ICICI is one of India’s largest banking giants that’s likely not going anywhere and can be held on to for some diversification of modest growth. For the short-term play I outlined, however, the West Asia crisis remains an ever-present unpredictable risk in the short term. Moreover, its medium-term outlook looks modest at best, as the RBI seems to be taking a highly dovish stand even as Indian consumers seem increasingly disillusioned with the idea of 6% returns that barely seem to be keeping up with the actual cost of living and increasing aspirations for consumption. Any RBI relaxation on consumer credit or lending for trading should provide relief and a rally in the current environment, but, barring such unexpected relief, ICICI still has some work to do to figure out where its growth will come from over the next half-decade.

data.title: Compass: Clarity Is Lacking, But I'm Bullish Anyway
data.description:
Back in late September of last year, I decided to dig into an interesting situation in the market. This involved news that Compass, Inc. (COMP) had agreed to acquire Anywhere Real Estate in a deal that resulted in a combined enterprise value of about $10 billion. At that time, I lauded the company for buying Anywhere Real Estate at a discount to what it was trading for. Plus, there was the opportunity to achieve significant annual cost synergies amounting to $225 million. This combination of factors made me bullish enough to rate the business a Buy. And whenever I assign such a rating, I am making the claim that the stock should outperform the broader market for the foreseeable future.

Since then, however, the firm has actually fallen short. Shares are up only 1.9%, which is worse than the 6.5% increase that the S&P 500 Index (SP500) saw over the same window of time. Because of the timing of the completion of this merger and a lack of additional data for management, there is some uncertainty regarding what the future might look like. We do have a few glimpses, but we are otherwise operating in the dark. Having said that, based on the data that's currently available, I remain confident in this business and my bullish call on it. So because of that, I believe that reaffirming it as a soft Buy makes sense.

Of course, this picture could change as new data becomes available. And it just so happens that, on May 5th, management should be announcing results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. The current expectation is for pro forma revenue to improve, though pro forma profitability might actually worsen. However, there are certain positive indicators that management has provided. And I do think investors would be wise to keep a close eye on things to see what the picture might turn out to look like. Because even though I am bullish about it, I am not married to that rating. I have no problem downgrading the stock if things don't go well.

Digging into Compass

If you are new to Compass, it's helpful to know that the company operates as a global real estate services company that functions not only in every major U.S. city but also across 120 countries and territories globally. Before its combination with Anywhere Real Estate, it operated its brokerage business and a franchise business, the latter of which fell under the Christie’s International Real Estate brand. The business served 39 states and Washington, DC, with more than 37,000 real estate professionals at its owned brokerage business, plus it had over 100 franchises spread across 50 different countries and territories.

In the chart above, as well as in the chart below, you can see important data about the company. The chart above shows revenue, profits, and cash flows for the 2023 through 2025 fiscal years. The overall trajectory for the business was solid. And in the chart below, you can see the number of transactions and the gross transaction value that the company achieved to capture these revenue figures. The fact that the business was still generating net losses was discouraging in and of itself. But the improvement in cash flow is certainly promising.

Normally, I would like to dig deeper into these numbers. After all, I believe that in a typical situation, historical results help to paint a picture so that we can understand what the future will look like. But because of the size of its merger with Anywhere Real Estate, historical data is not as important as what the future for the business should be now that it is an entirely different entity in terms of overall substance. The fact of the matter is that combining with Anywhere Real Estate brought the company-owned brokerage operations under the Coldwell Banker, Better Homes and Gardens Real Estate, Sotheby’s International Realty, and other names. Anywhere Real Estate offered a full-service real estate brokerage business, and it also provided customers with full-service title, escrow, and settlement services.

On January 9th, management announced that the two companies had completed their merger. The good news is that while this means that we will miss out on some revenue and profitability from Anywhere Real Estate for the first few days of the year, the vast majority of its financial performance will be captured. Based on my own calculations, following the completion of the deal, Compass should now have net debt of around $2.25 billion. That is substantially higher than the net cash position it had before this of $176.3 million. But as I mentioned already, management expects significant benefits from this maneuver.

In my previous article, I detailed how the combined business should generate around $592 million of EBITDA. However, that assumed that management would be right about profitability for both businesses for 2025. Although we don't know how Anywhere Real Estate performed, we do know that Compass generated $293.4 million worth of EBITDA. That compares to the $242 million that management had estimated. This means that actual EBITDA will likely be closer to $643.4 million for the combined companies on a pro forma basis. And in all probability, adjusted operating cash flow would be somewhere around $401 million. If management confirms that synergies will still be at $225 million, then it's not unthinkable that adjusted operating cash flow could grow to $626 million while EBITDA could expand to $868.4 million.

This makes it surprisingly simple to value the company. In the chart above, you can see how the business is valued both with synergies and without them. Without synergies, the business looks decent, but not fantastic. But with them, it is definitely a value play. The great thing here is that, given the rapid growth that Compass had experienced in recent years, guidance for 2026 could be strong enough to make the stock even cheaper. Speaking of guidance, while management has not provided details regarding 2026 in its entirety, they did say that the first quarter should see revenue of between $2.55 billion and $2.75 billion. And if we adjust for certain one-time events associated with this merger, EBITDA should range between $32.5 million and $47.5 million.

In the table above, you can see calculations using results for the first quarter of 2025 for both Compass and Anywhere Real Estate. This shows that, on a combined basis, the company last year generated sales of about $2.56 billion. We can also get a pretty good read on what profitability would be. And when you factor in the change in share count, we would be looking at a loss per share for the first quarter of last year amounting to $0.17.

The table below, meanwhile, shows what analysts think compared to what these pro forma calculations demonstrated for the company. They are of the opinion that revenue will be somewhere around $2.67 billion, while the loss per share should come in at about $0.18. That would mean a worsening in profitability from a loss of $128.7 million to a loss of $133.6 million. But with the overall growth in EBITDA from $14.6 million to $40 million at the midpoint, I wouldn't be surprised if cash flows for the business moved higher.

In addition to getting better guidance on what the future might look like, especially when it comes to projected cost savings, one thing that I really hope is that management will provide detail on another development that just came out. On April 15th, Compass entered into an agreement to acquire a 51% ownership stake in some undisclosed company whereby it will, over the span of 30 months, make payments toward that entity. There is a lot of ambiguity regarding this transaction. But what we do know is that it is an entity that owns franchisees operating under the Sotheby’s International Realty brand. Conceptually, this should expand the business. And, according to the terms of the agreement, the fact that it could be forced to acquire the remaining 49% equity stake could mean additional expansion. But until management provides more details on the matter, we are really left in the dark here.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, I believe that Compass is an interesting company that deserves attention. I don't think that it is the best opportunity on the market right now. However, when you look at what the company is becoming and how it's priced, it is certainly attractive enough to justify a Buy rating. My only complaint is that we don't yet have enough detail to have a better picture of what the situation looks like. But as new data comes in, I suspect that it will confirm that further upside is warranted.

data.title: Grab: The Market Got This One Wrong
data.description:
Investment Summary

Let me start this article off by saying that I may sound biased because I'm a first-hand customer/consumer of Grab's (GRAB) products and services, but this doesn't change the fact that this is a good company, not only boasting profits and achieving management targets, but I can see, first-hand, its positive effects of giving people jobs and merchants to participate in a competitive market, which is why I think it's a very interesting buy.

It's commonly known for ride-hailing and food delivery services, but it has embedded itself in the ecosystem that they now also offer financial services.

Another part that makes me more positive on Grab is the strength of its user base. Like what I've said above, as a consumer, Grab really is a good platform, but it's also cool to see strong activity and growth in transaction volume across other Southeast Asian countries, which tells me people are using the app more often, not just spending more per order.

GrabFin is another reason I'm leaning bullish since they're able to really embed themselves in the ecosystem by being more involved in every consumer's way of life. Need food or groceries? Deliveries? And this time, do you need a loan? Grab can finally offer that to you.

Naturally, their financial services segment still has room to grow, and if it reaches breakeven as management expects, that could become a major margin driver for the whole company.

I also think the balance sheet gives Grab some breathing room, especially in a time where oil prices have significantly increased in SEA countries and a probable reduction of consumer spending. Having a strong cash position lowers the downside and gives the company flexibility to keep investing in growth, buybacks, and new opportunities.

That is why I do not believe that the stock is risk-free, because things like regulation in Indonesia, competitive food delivery, and even fuel cost pressure are all real things that may slow the story and, overall, slow down the company's growth.

In the end, I think that Grab is a Strong Buy. I believe that the market might continue to underestimate the combination of improving profitability, and financial services upside as well as the underpinning of its cash position. The thesis, in my case, can be reduced to the following: Grab is beginning to resemble a true operating business with a variety of growth drivers rather than a high-tech narrative with high beta.

The Business Behind the Ticker

To people who have not heard of Grab (GRAB), the company can be summarized as the answer to all things digital in Southeast Asia. What began as a Malaysian ride-hailing application in 2012 has since expanded to an eight-country superapp platform (including Singapore, Malaysia, Indonesia, Thailand, Vietnam, the Philippines, Cambodia, and Myanmar) with a collective population of more than 670 million people. The platform has 4 major revenue engines:

Mobility includes ride-hailing, including the economy four- and two-wheel rides, as well as premium services to the airport.

Deliveries encompass GrabFood, where restaurants deliver their meals; GrabMart, where groceries and convenience items are delivered; and GrabExpress, where parcels are delivered.

Grab makes a commission on each transaction, which is usually 16-25% of GMV based on the market (and varies between countries), and the economic engine is enhanced as more people ride in a city, as shorter delivery routes and reduced fulfillment costs.

Financial Services under the GrabFin brand include GrabPay digital payments, consumer lending, buy-now-pay-later, micro-investments via Earn+, insurance, and full digital banking licenses in Singapore, Malaysia, and Indonesia. The loan book had a gross of more than $1.3 billion in 2025, and the management has now set the target of doubling it by the end of 2026.

Finally, Others, in which Grab works at an ecosystem layer, including GrabAds merchant and brand monetization and Partner Apps, launched in October 2025, which incorporates third-party travel and service providers directly into the Grab interface and adds utility to a session without the need to operate.

The flywheel argument is easy to understand: the greater the number of transactions, the greater the amount of behavioral information, and enhanced data enhances GrabFin underwriting and product customization. Better products are related to more users and more engagement. More GMV is created with more engagement, resulting in more revenue and expanded margins.

What the Numbers Actually Mean

Q4 2025 revenue came in at $906 million, up 19% year-over-year, bringing the full year to $3.37 billion (up 20% from 2024's $2.80 billion). Beneath the surface, it is not as dirty as the headline suggests.

On-Demand GMV of $6.10 billion in Q4, increased by 21% Y/OY. However, the more significant measure was transaction volume, which increased 24% (beating GMV growth by 300 basis points).

As the transactions increase at rates higher than GMV, it is an indication that users are placing orders more often at the same or slightly smaller basket sizes.

Monthly transacting users reached 50.5 million in Q4, an increase of 15% compared to a year ago, and GMV per MTU increased 4% to $131. The deeper addressable pool is the 129 million annual transacting user base, and the top-of-funnel opportunity is truly huge, with only 37% of the user base penetrated with MTU-to-ATU. Grab now covers 1 in 15 people in Southeast Asia, as President & COO Alexander Charles Hungate explained on the Q4 2025 earnings call. For context, two years back, it was 1 in 20.

The GrabFin figures are noteworthy. Q4 Financial Services revenue increased 34% year-over-year to a high of $99 million, and the gross loan portfolio grew 120% to $1.18 billion compared to $536 million a year ago.

As I've mentioned in the previous section, management reported that the full-year loan book was $1.3 billion as of 2025, a major improvement on the earlier $1 billion forecast, and they hope to have $2 billion by the end of 2026.

As mentioned in the Q4 2025 earnings call, these 3 digital banks now have a deposit base of 7.4 million customers in just 3 years. And nearly all of them were converted to the platform instead of being acquired via paid marketing, which is directly connected to the cost advantage of embedded financial services distribution.

Full-year adjusted EBITDA increased 60%, and adjusted free cash flow increased 128% to $500 million and $290 million, respectively, in 2024. Corporate costs, as CFO Peter Oey noted in the Q&A of the Q4 2025 earnings call, decreased to 17% of revenue in 2023 and to 11% in 2025, a 600-basis-point decline over three years, driven by AI efficiency and headcount leverage.

The Math Behind the Thesis

The cleaner way to value Grab is through enterprise value, not market capitalization, because the balance sheet materially changes what investors are actually paying for the operating business.

As of December 31, 2025, Grab reported $7.4 billion of gross cash liquidity and $5.4 billion of net cash liquidity, giving it one of the stronger balance sheets among consumer internet platforms of comparable scale. That matters because a meaningful portion of the company's equity value is backed by cash rather than by expectations embedded in the operating business itself.

What needs to happen, and is it realistic?

The 2028 EBITDA target of management of $1.5 billion needs a 20% CAGR of the 2025 base of $3.37 billion, which means that the company needs to generate revenue of approximately $5.8 billion and a 25.9% EBITDA margin. That represents an 11 percentage point growth in three years. There are three things that must be right to make that happen.

First, on-demand margins should continue to trend towards their long-term goals. Mobility already stands at approximately 8.6% EBITDA margin on GMV, which is close to the 9%+ target. Deliveries are at around 2% and should be 4%+.

Again, this may be a bit difficult to achieve, especially with macroeconomic headwinds around oil looming around causing ride-hailing riders & drivers, and food delivery workers to generate lesser income (because they cover their own fuel expenses), and consumers alike to think twice about ordering food or other services online, just generally a much more hesitant consumer base with SEA being one of the most affected countries with the Iran oil shock.

But at the same time I'm betting that things will work out for Grab because even if the stock tanks a bit, once the economy recovers, you're essentially acquiring Grab at a discount, which is already a big player (and still can be the biggest superapp) in SEA countries.

The path there is fulfillment density, AI-enhanced reduction of dispatch costs, and GrabMart growing out of its existing 10% of Deliveries GMV. GrabMart is already expanding 1.7x faster than GrabFood, and GrabMart users increased 30% per year in 2025, as per the previously mentioned Q4 2025 call.

Second, and most importantly, GrabFin needs to reach breakeven in H2 2026. This saw an increase in Financial Services revenue of 37% in 2025 to $347 million, the gross loan portfolio reached 1.18 billion, and the management is aiming at $2 billion by year-end 2026. Once GrabFin turns around, it ceases to drag group margins and starts producing high-margin interest revenue on a soaring loan book.

That is the largest EBITDA unlock in the model, and, given the trend of the loan book and the commitment made by the CFO on record, I also believe that this is very likely. With how rough the economy has been (as a person living in the Philippines), although it's not too optimistic to think about it in a holistic sense, having access to loan apps/loan services, especially if you're in the middle-to-low class in society with friendlier rates, would be beneficial for society.

Third, corporate costs should grow more slowly than revenue. They already are, with SG&A falling from 116% of revenue in 2021 to 24.5% in 2025. R&D fell to 12.7% in comparison to 52.7%. The percentage of corporate expenses relative to revenue declined by 600 basis points in only two years. The efficiency of headcount enabled by AI enabled Grab to expand its city coverage and minimized the number of operations headcount. This trend is on the upswing, and I do not see any structural reason why it'd go down.

The Implied Price

With discounted free cash flow in three years at a 10% discount rate, FCF conversion increases to 58% in 2025, up to the 80% target of the management in 2028, and results in an approximation of $462 million in 2026, $792 million in 2027, and $1.2 billion in 2028.

The terminal EV is $30 billion at a multiple of 20x of the 2028 EBITDA of 1.5 billion – conservative compared to the 20x to 25x of the historical Sea Limited (SE) and reasonable considering the projected margin and growth profile of Grab. When you discount all the cash flows and subtract the net cash of $5.37 billion, you get an implied equity value of $29.9 billion, or $7.31 per share – 82% higher than the current $4.02.

A skeptic using 18x rather than 20x will have $6.70 per share (still a 67% upside). The net cash position alone would provide a valuation floor, although in a scenario where GrabFin breakeven slides into 2027 and Deliveries margins have a more gradual improvement, this is the valuation floor that the market seems to be ignoring entirely.

What The Street Says

This is not an isolated opinion. Out of 27 analysts who cover Grab on TIKR, 19 of them give it a Buy rating, 7 of them give it an Outperform rating, and only 1 of them gives it a Hold rating – 96% bullish. The average price is $6.31, the median is $6.28, and even the most conservative active analyst has the stock at 4.80 -20% higher than today.

Even if we head to Grab's page on Seeking Alpha, it shows a Buy rating from SA Analysts with a Strong Buy from Wall Street Analysts' Ratings, which tells me that I'm not alone in this notion that Grab is a good company and can potentially grow bigger in the future.

My implied price of $7.31 is above the street price but below the high target of $8.00, which I believe is the appropriate place to hit based on the same management guidance that the street is operating under, but with credit to the breakeven inflection of GrabFin and the compounding effect of FCF conversion expansion through 2028.

With a 15.6x forward EV/EBITDA on 42% guided EBITDA growth, a net cash of $5.4 billion, and a fintech segment about to experience the most significant earnings inflection, the stock appears to be mispriced. The rating is Buy.

Catalysts Worth Watching, and By When

The nearest financial trigger is the attainment of the EBITDA breakeven by GrabFin in H2 2026. This was simply put by CFO & Director Peter Oey in the Q4 2025 earnings transcript:

"...we are firmly on track to achieve EBITDA breakeven in the second half of 2026."

- CFO & Director Peter Oey

The segment will turn into an asset, not a liability, in case the book of loans doubles to $2 billion by the end of 2026, as projected, and the cost of credit remains in the present risk appetite, which the management projects the risk-adjusted returns will be above the cost of capital. That inflection is the sole way to shift the group EBITDA trend as of 2027 to leave the current consensus with the full picture not yet fully captured.

The second catalyst is GrabMart, which is likely to be underestimated. It now only constitutes 10% of Deliveries GMV even though it is increasing 1.7x quicker than GrabFood. The current penetration of retail in Southeast Asia is less than 40%, and online grocery is less than 3% in most markets.

A 2028 financial catalyst is the Taiwan migration, which is expected to be completed by early 2027. The foodpanda acquisition provides the first move by Grab outside of Southeast Asia, into a market of 23 million individuals with an already operating food delivery business at adjusted EBITDA profitability. The $1.5 billion organic target does not include management-guided at least $60 million of incremental EBITDA in 2028 and, therefore, is additive.

The acquisition of the Stash investing platform, which will be closed in Q3 or Q4 2026, is also not included in organic guidance and will add more than $60 million in 2028 EBITDA to the suite of financial services currently lacking at Grab, filling in a wealth management offering.

The Ai.R autonomous vehicle program is a longer-duration catalyst, yet a real one. It went public in the Punggol district of Singapore on April 1, 2026, and already registered 30,000 kilometers with zero safety-critical incidents, the highest autonomous miles of any AV operator in Southeast Asia.

The economic impact in the long term is straightforward: no commission to drivers on a ride will fundamentally reshape the Mobility P/L. Singapore is the "blueprint", and management has stated point-blank that lessons learned in Punggol will be employed in expansion throughout the region in the coming three years.

Risks and What Could Go Wrong

I would rather be straightforward, since there are actual dangers to the thesis.

The most significant one is Indonesia. Grab has a big market in Indonesia, and any backflushing on the issue of the commission cap (the biggest number is cutting commissions from 20% to 10%, which is good for the riders/drivers, not so good for Grab) – should political pressure on driver welfare play out as binding policy and not a consultative discussion, it would squeeze the take rates in a market that Grab cannot afford to lose. This is the risk that I am monitoring the most. In the event that that story alters in a material way over the following two quarters, the thesis will be subject to re-evaluation.

Food delivery competition, especially with the ShopeeFood offered by Sea Limited, is a reality. This does not mean that the structural benefit that Grab enjoys in terms of logistics density can stop a properly capitalized competitor from conducting promotional campaigns that undermine user loyalty in cost-sensitive markets. When Grab is coerced into a protracted incentive raise to protect GMV share, the story of the expansion of the EBITDA margin decelerates. But on a personal note, at least in the Philippines, ShopeeFood is nowhere near Grab's dominance; they're basically the SEA version of the legendary Uber (UBER).

Then there is the energy picture, which affects all parties: Grab as a company, merchants thinking twice if they'll still conduct business, riders & drivers considering other professions that offer higher margins, and fearful consumers over this macroeconomic pressure. High prices of fuel are a direct threat to the economics of drivers and delivery partners at Grab.

By April 8, 2026, when President Trump announced a ceasefire, gasoline prices in the U.S. had already surged over $4.14 per gallon, more than a dollar higher than they were before the conflict, and Southeast Asian markets, which heavily relied on the Strait of Hormuz transit to import refined fuel, were already handling emergency conservation measures. The increased fuel prices decrease drivers' real earnings per trip, straining supply, and providing political incentives to reduce commissions.

The Strait of Hormuz is completely open at the time of writing, with the presence of the U.S. Navy over the course of the ceasefire, and crude oil dropped at the announcement of Brent to about $90.38 (down 9.07%) and WTI to about $83.85 (11.45%).

The two-week conditional ceasefire between the U.S. and Iran has brought short-term relief to the energy markets, and analysts estimate a national average gasoline price that will fall 5 to 15 cents per gallon in the following weeks.

There is doubt as to whether the ceasefire will last and go beyond two weeks. I do not base my case on a modeling of a fuel cost windfall, but the directional change is positive to the health of the driver supply in Grab and decreases the severity of what was an otherwise significant operational headwind in the near term.

The Bottom Line

This thesis is anchored on a 16-quarter streak of EBITDA improvement, a first-ever GAAP net profit year, a CFO who publicly vowed to make GrabFin breakeven by H2 2026, a 129 million annual transacting user base with only a 37% activation rate per month, and a stock price that remains almost 30% below its 2025 highs. Despite that, the market has valued Grab like it is the pre-profitable version of the same; it is not.

The bottom is the $5.4 billion of net cash that renders the downside to be manageable. It is 36% of total market capitalization, it is generating a payoff, and it is being deployed with discipline into buybacks at nearly 52-week lows, into the Taiwan foodpanda acquisition at an attractive 0.33x entry price of GMV, and into Stash, the free-cash-flow-positive investing platform that fills the one apparent gap in the GrabFin product portfolio and will add $60 million.

Importantly, neither Taiwan nor Stash is reflected in the management's $1.5 billion organic EBITDA guidance, implying that they are a positive addition to the model and not assumptions that have to be carried to the model.

I find myself returning to what CFO & Director Peter Oey said at the end of the earnings call. This is the second occasion in the history of Grab that the management has provided three-year guidance.

The initial one was in September 2022, and the entirety of the factors the management had been developing towards over two years finally came to fruition in 2025, and they wanted the shareholders to know where the business is going, not merely where it has been.

I believe it is real. I see Grab's effect on my local areas and how they've provided jobs for people while making sure it's a win-win environment for merchants to participate in. The operating data supports it, the balance sheet protects the downside, and the valuation at 15.6x forward EV/EBITDA on 42% EBITDA growth leaves meaningful room to be patient. The rating is Buy.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Allspring Common Stock Fund: Q1 2026 Top Contributors And Detractors
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Allspring Common Stock Fund Q1 2026 Commentary.

The Allspring Common Stock Fund underperformed the Russell 2500 Index during the period. Performance was mainly driven by negative stock selection, with sector allocation also having a slight negative effect on overall results. Health care was the most significant detractor, led by weakness within life sciences tools and services, while consumer discretionary and IT also weighed on relative performance. Financials was the largest contributor, supported by strong stock selection within capital markets and select banking names.

The broader market backdrop was mixed during the period. Strength in cyclical areas such as energy and industrials reflected improving optimism around industrial activity and higher commodity prices, while smaller- and mid-cap growth-oriented sectors faced pressure from elevated interest rates, volatility in IT, and heightened scrutiny of earnings visibility. Within this environment, the portfolio's exposure to select financials provided relative support but was outweighed by declines in several individual holdings.

Stocks Contributing to Relative Performance Include:

Littelfuse, Inc. (LFUS), designs and manufactures circuit protection, sensing, and power control products serving electronics, automotive, and industrial end markets. LFUS delivered strong performance in the quarter as results and guidance exceeded expectations, supported by stronger bookings (B/B > 1 and bookings up >20% year over year) and solid backlog entering 2026, which drove improved operating leverage. Performance was led by the electronics segment, where growth tied to data center, grid, utilities, and renewable applications more than offset softness in residential HVAC, commercial vehicles, and certain areas of power semiconductors. Management highlighted increasing encouragement around early signs of industrial recovery and continued outgrowth in automotive while previewing portfolio actions ahead of a planned May analyst day, including deeper integration of recent acquisitions and a sharper focus on higher-value semiconductor applications. Data center and AI-related demand were also cited as areas of increasing strategic importance as power architectures grow more complex.

Regal Rexnord Corp. (RRX) engineers and manufactures factory automation sub-systems, industrial powertrain solutions, automation and mechanical power transmission components, electric motors and electronic controls, air moving products, and specialty electrical components and systems. RRX performed strongly in the first quarter, primarily on account of 1) a quarterly report that featured particularly strong orders owing to budding demand for RRX electrical products sold into the data center market, which drives a substantial revision to 2027 growth expectations, and 2) two >50 Institute for Supply Management Manufacturing Purchasing Manager's Index prints following three years of sub-50 conditions. We consider RRX to be an attractive security offering exposure to improving prospects for growth in U.S. manufacturing investment, with an expanding side opportunity for growing its small data center electrical business.

Westlake Corp. (WLK) is a global diversified chemical and building products company that engages in the manufacturing and supplying of PVC, caustic soda, polyethylene, and various building products. WLK outperformed in the first quarter due to the effects on global chemical markets from the conflict in Iran, which has had an extreme impact on the supply of global chemical products. Various competing global chemical producers have declared unforeseen circumstances or had their input costs materially inflate, while WLK's operations and costs have been largely unaffected, as its facilities are located in the U.S. and thus have access to unaffected U.S. natural gas supplies. The situation highlights WLK's core advantage pertaining to its place on the global cost curve and security of operations. WLK's separate building products company brings exposure to eventual improvements off of depressed levels in home improvement spending and construction activity.

Kirby Corp. (KEX) provides inland and coastal marine transportation services for bulk liquid products and distributes equipment and services to energy and industrial customers. KEX performed well in the quarter as marine transportation results improved through the period, with inland barge utilization recovering meaningfully by quarter-end and margins coming in better than expected. While pricing softened modestly during the quarter, management characterized the decline as temporary, citing favorable renewal activity and continued capacity discipline. KEX has also benefited from power generation needs from data centers in their engines segment and should benefit from any commodity production increases in the U.S. to offset shut-in capacity globally related to the Iran conflict.

Stocks Detracting from Relative Performance Include:

Azenta, Inc. (AZTA), provides biological and chemical sample management, genomics, and laboratory automation solutions to research and clinical customers, generating revenue through service contracts and consumables. AZTA underperformed the benchmark during the quarter as profitability weakened, with adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) margins pressured by underused lab capacity, Automated Stores remediation costs, and softer demand in portions of Sample Management Solutions. Organic revenue in Sample Management declined modestly, reflecting continued challenges in the Automated Stores and Cryo businesses, while earnings fell year over year despite modest topline growth. AZTA completed the acquisition of UK Biocentre, which is expected to dilute margins in fiscal 2026 but expands European biorepository capabilities and supports longer-term growth objectives.

Herc Holdings, Inc. (HRI), provides equipment rental solutions to construction, industrial, and infrastructure customers, generating revenue primarily from fleet rentals and related services. HRI performed poorly during the quarter as investor attention centered on elevated leverage and near-term execution risk despite solid fourth-quarter results and rental demand. Management spent the period focused on integrating the H&E Equipment Services acquisition, including branch conversions and sales territory optimization that added operational complexity early in the year. The company also implemented a new regional operating structure to support future scale and efficiency gains, though benefits have yet to be reflected in sentiment. Overall, the quarter highlighted steady underlying demand but ongoing integration and balance sheet considerations that weighed on shares.

Planet Fitness, Inc. (PLNT), operates a franchised and company-owned fitness center network, generating revenue from memberships, franchise royalties, and equipment sales. Shares fell after cautious full-year guidance signaled slower sales and earnings growth, despite strong quarterly performance. While revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and earnings exceeded expectations, management highlighted near-term headwinds, including weaker join trends early in the year, higher churn in January, and a more back-half-weighted club-opening schedule. Additional pressure came from lower expected equipment sales and incremental cost headwinds that tempered the outlook for fiscal 2026. The quarter reflected steady brand demand and continued unit growth, but investor sentiment shifted toward normalization after several years of outsized growth.

Revolve Group, Inc. (RVLV), operates an online fashion platform selling apparel, footwear, and beauty products to a global, digitally native customer base. RVLV had lackluster performance during the quarter as investor reaction centered on higher planned marketing spend and a more cautious margin outlook, despite solid revenue growth and improving customer engagement. Results showed accelerating demand, supported by broad-based category growth and increased active customer additions, but near-term profitability expectations were tempered as the company leaned into brand investment. Management also continued to expand its own brand portfolio and beauty offering, which contributed to growth but weighed modestly on average order values. Overall, the quarter reflected strong topline execution while near-term expense discipline remained the primary focus for investors.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

For additional detailed fund characteristics data, please visit our web site, Allspring Global Investments: Investment Management & Services, self identify on the landing page, then click Investments and Mutual Funds via the top navigation.

Portfolio holdings and characteristics are subject to change and may have changed since the date specified. The holdings listed should not be considered recommendations to purchase or sell a particular security.

Benchmark Descriptions:

The Russell 2500™ Index measures the performance of the small-to mid-cap segment of the U.S. equity universe. It is a subset of the Russell 3000® Index and includes approximately 2,500 of the smallest securities based on a combination of their market capitalization and current index membership. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The S&P 500 Index consists of 500 stocks chosen for market size, liquidity, and industry group representation. It is a market-value-weighted index with each stock's weight in the index proportionate to its market value. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Institute of Supply Management (ISM) Manufacturing Purchasing Managers Index (PMI) is an index of the prevailing direction of economic trends in the United States manufacturing and service sectors. It consists of a diffusion index that summarizes whether market conditions, as viewed by purchasing managers, are expanding, staying the same, or contracting. The purpose of the PMI is to provide information about current and future business conditions to company decision makers, analysts, and investors. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Lipper averages are compiled by Lipper, Inc., an independent mutual fund research and rating service. Each Lipper average represents a universe of funds that are similar in investment objective. You cannot invest directly in a Lipper average.

The Morningstar Category average is the average return for the peer group based on the returns of each individual fund within the group. The total return of the Morningstar Category average does not include the effect of sales charges. You cannot invest directly in a Morningstar Category average.

Diversification does not ensure or guarantee better performance and cannot eliminate the risk of investment losses.

Attribution Analysis:

Performance attribution and sector returns are calculated using the Brinson-Fachler attribution model. As such, performance attribution calculations may differ from the fund's actual investment results. Common sources of attribution dispersion from official returns include: the use of end of day holdings and prices, the exclusion of fees, and the use of myriad pricing sources to accommodate different benchmark providers.

Definition of Terms:

Alpha measures the difference between a fund's actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Downside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of negative returns. During periods of negative benchmark returns, a downside capture ratio less than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and a downside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Information ratio measures the consistency of excess return (return in excess of a benchmark). This value is determined by taking the annualized excess return over a benchmark (style benchmark by default) and dividing it by the standard deviation of excess return.

R-squared is a measurement of how similar a fund's historical performance has been to that of the benchmark. The measure ranges from 0.0, which means that the fund's performance bears no relationship to the performance of the index, to 1.0, which means that the fund's performance was perfectly synchronized with the performance of the benchmark.

Sharpe ratio measures the potential reward offered by a mutual fund relative to its risk level. The ratio uses a fund's standard deviation and its excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. The higher the Sharpe ratio, the better the fund's historical risk-adjusted performance.

Standard deviation represents the degree to which an investment's performance has varied from its average performance over a particular time period.

Tracking error measures the extent to which a manager's performance mimics that of a benchmark. The value is the standard deviation of the difference between a fund's performance and a benchmark's performance.

Upside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of positive returns. During periods of positive benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Risks:

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Stock values fluctuate in response to the activities of individual companies and general market and economic conditions. Smaller company stocks tend to be more volatile and less liquid than those of larger companies. Consult the fund's prospectus for additional information on these and other risks.

The Morningstar Rating for funds, or star rating, is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar risk-adjusted return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36–59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60–119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods.
