data.title: April's 5 Dividend Growth Stocks With Yields Up To 8.16%
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman

For some background on this monthly publication, here is my view on dividend growth stocks:

Dividend growth stocks aren't always the most exciting investments out there. They often aren't grabbing the headlines, and they aren't the stocks running up hundreds of percentages in a year. In fact, they are often some of the least exciting stocks. And that is precisely their strongest selling point. With such a vast world of dividend growth stocks available out there, it is important to screen through to see if there are any worthwhile investments to explore.

They are stocks that provide growing wealth over time to income investors. Dividend growers are often larger (not always), more financially stable companies that can pay out reliable cash flows to investors. Some are slower growers than others. Some are going to be cyclical that require a strong economy. Some are going to be secular, which doesn't generally rely on a more robust economy.

Dividend growth can promote share price appreciation. Of course, that is if these companies are growing their earnings to support such dividend growth in the first place. Trust me. There are yield traps out there - I've owned a few that I'm not particularly proud of.

I like to think of investing in dividend stocks as a perpetual loan of sorts. Essentially, every dividend is a repayment of your original capital. Eventually, holding long enough, you have the position "paid off." It is all returned back into your pocket from that point forward.

All of this being said, it is important to understand my approach to dividend stocks and why screening dividend stocks can be important for income investors. As with any initial screening, this is just an initial dive - more due diligence would be necessary before pulling the trigger.

The Parameters For Screening

I'll be using some handy features that Seeking Alpha provides right here on their website for this screen. In particular, I will be screening utilizing their quant grades in dividend safety, dividend growth and dividend consistency.

Dividend Safety is relatively self-explanatory. These will be stocks that SA quants show reasonable safety compared to the rest of their various sectors. The grade considers many different factors, but earnings payout ratios, debt and free cash flow are among these. This category will be stocks with A+ to B- ratings.

For the dividend growth category, we have factors such as the CAGR of various periods relative to other stocks in the same sector. Additionally, the quants also look at earnings, revenue and EBITDA growth. As we will see, this doesn't mean that every stock with a higher grade has the growth we are looking for. This just factors in that the dividend has grown or earnings are growing to support possible dividend growth. For these, the grades will also be A+ through B- ratings.

Finally, for dividend consistency, we want stocks that will be paying reliable dividends for us for a very long time. In particular, hopefully, they are raising yearly, though that isn't an explicit requirement. We will also include stocks with a general uptrend in dividend payments, which means there could have been periods where they paused increases for a year or two.

After looking at those factors alone, we are left with 309 stocks at this time, which is up just a bit from last month's 302. However, the highest yield is also down from over 8% for the top-ranked previously.

I'll link the screen here, though it is a dynamic list that constantly updates regularly. When viewing this article, there could be more or less when going to the link. As prices fluctuate, the dividend yields can also change from the time of writing as well.

From there, I wanted to narrow down the list a lot more. I then sorted the list by TTM dividend yield, from highest to lowest. Since these will be safer dividend stocks in the first place, screening for those among the higher payers shouldn't hurt. Though there are always exceptions, after all, this is simply an initial screening, and I try to note those that I feel aren't as safe as the algorithms are suggesting.

As is usually the case, we have a number of names that are repeats from month to month. In our highlights today, we'll be skipping over T. Rowe Price (TROW) and Kimberly-Clark (KMB) since we covered them within the last month.

With that, it leaves us with RLJ Lodging Trust (RLJ), VICI Properties (VICI), Best Buy Co (BBY), NNN REIT (NNN) and The Clorox Company (CLX).

#1 RLJ Lodging Trust 8.16%

This is a name that has shown up for us many times now over the last year; it is also a name that I consider relatively riskier to consider. While the algorithm gives it a high grade on dividend safety, I'd be a bit more cautious. As a hotel and resort-focused REIT, this name is going to be quite sensitive to overall economic conditions. They are also focused on higher-end premium rooms with a limited portfolio of 92 hotels. With an enterprise value of only around $3.4 billion, this is quite a small REIT as well.

During Covid they ended up slashing their dividend meaningfully and held it at a token level for a number of years. They then started to raise it aggressively over the last couple of years.

This is on the back of weakening AFFO, and it doesn't look like analysts are too optimistic looking forward either.

With a net debt to EBITDA of around 6x, they are pushing the elevated levels even for REITs. If earnings don't improve, this then starts to look even worse.

On a more optimistic note, though, the AFFO payout ratio based on 2026 estimates comes in at ~44%. That is on the very low side relative to most REITs, leaving them plenty of room and potentially time to hopefully come up with a turnaround plan while leaving the dividend intact. Further, it comes in with a dirt-cheap forward P/AFFO of 5.4x.

Overall, I would consider this a more speculative consideration for investors.

#2 VICI Properties 6.44% Yield

VICI continues to be a name that I find attractively valued, but one that is continually pressured given the challenges it is currently facing. This is a Las Vegas casino-focused REIT, but they have been expanding into other types of experiential properties. It is primarily the decline in tourism to the strip that has been an overhang. While the REIT simply collects the rent checks, mostly sheltering it from the economic ebbs and flows, too much pressure on tenants can lead to trouble for the landlord.

They are highly concentrated in primarily Caesars and MGM Resorts, representing over 70% of their annualized rent. So despite some diversification efforts, they still command an overwhelming amount of exposure.

Still, I have faith that VICI will remain fine, considering they had already weathered through Covid. Albeit a rather brief but ultimately type of worst-case scenario that played out. Even though growth is expected to slow down for VICI, analysts are still expecting growth going forward, despite these challenges as well.

That should continue to help support the growing payout that they've been delivering to investors.

I'm currently long shares of VICI from writing puts previously at $32.50, and I have another short put trade at the $27.50 strike price. Suffice it to say, I find this name attractive to consider for investors who don't mind a bit of a riskier REIT play.

#3 Best Buy Co 5.91% Yield

This is only the second time that BBY has made the discussion portion of our monthly piece. The name had been facing earnings pressure as EPS had stagnated for a number of years after electronics demand waned after covid. They also have to face ever-increasing competitive pressures of online shopping from the likes of Amazon (AMZN).

Though optimistically, analysts are starting to expect a turnaround and earnings growth to resume once again. However, even in the more optimistic scenario of earnings growth starting back up, the company isn't expected to hit those levels again over the next five years.

They've also continued to increase their dividend to investors annually, at the token 1 penny increase to keep the streak alive. This brings them up to 21 years of consecutive dividend increases.

Based on this fiscal year's estimates, the payout ratio comes in at just shy of 60%. Should they experience the growth analysts estimate, then this starts to ease payout pressures even further—even if we assume a penny raise in each of the coming years.

At its current forward P/E of ~10x, investors aren't too enthusiastic, as this is a relatively cheap valuation for this name.

Should they meet the estimates of analysts or even exceed them, I could see BBY being a great bargain here as the share price likely does head significantly higher in the coming years. Thus, the nearly 6% yield now makes it look quite enticing while waiting for that potential turnaround.

#4 NNN REIT 5.56% Yield

This is another name that I currently own in the REIT space, and I continue to find it attractive here. In fact, I had been writing puts as a way to potentially grow my position at the $40 strike level. We are at a price where that could start making sense again to continue with writing puts—potentially even just adding some shares outright.

As a retail-focused REIT, this name has faced pressures from a higher rate environment. Given the fact that it is expected that the Fed is likely not going to be cutting rates further this year, and risk-free Treasury rates have been rising, we are once again seeing those pressures play out. There has even been some chatter that the Fed may be pushed into a position to possibly raise its benchmark rate as well on the back of surging oil prices.

That's to be determined, but that has all put pressure on NNN. When rates started heading higher in 2022/2023, we can see that the multiple for this name started facing pressure quite meaningfully. More recently, we are seeing that again and at 12x forward P/AFFO, I think it is looking quite attractive.

They have a long history of raising their payout each year, going on 35 years now. With that sort of commitment, I don't see that ending anytime soon.

Given the AFFO payout ratio of 67.6%, they aren't in a position of straining to support their dividend either. That's on top of the fact that earnings are expected to continue to trend higher. It's certainly a slow grower, but this isn't one of those names that is expected to be overly exciting either. It offers a decent yield that grows over the long term.

For an income investor, I believe that NNN continues to remain a strong candidate for adding at this time.

#5 The Clorox Company 4.88% Yield

CLX is a household name that probably needs little introduction, but it is a name that has never made the discussion portion of our pieces either.

Over the last year, the share price is down over 30%, and that has pushed its yield higher—though they've also been raising their payout consistently as well. In fact, this is a name that is almost a dividend king. A company that has been able to raise its payout for 50+ years, and CLX is at year 48.

While I'm sure they'll achieve the dividend king crown and keep pushing higher, it isn't without some risks to consider. This is a consume staples stock, but its earnings have been quite volatile over the years. Interestingly, similar to BBY, they had a strong earnings growth tied to Covid, but then that collapsed after.

Now, heading into fiscal 2026, earnings are expected to come under significant pressure again. On a brighter note, they are supposed to rebound and start trending higher once again heading into 2027. That said, if they come in at the $5.92, that would push their payout ratio to 84%. A very elevated level for a company like this.

Revenue growth has been limited, with them noting they expect net sales to be down 6-10% this fiscal year. They also highlighted that EPS is expected to decrease from 9-14% year-over-year, with a "negative impact of about 90 cents related to the reversal of the impact from incremental shipments associated with the ERP transition..." Which helps to highlight at least partially why earnings are expected to drop so sharply this year but then rebound, as this is a one-time event.

Despite the sharp decline in the share price, the forward P/E comes in at 15.3x.

Historically, that is incredibly low and would otherwise be a cheap valuation. However, I'm not sure investors will care, given it is not necessarily a dirt-cheap valuation on an absolute basis. It also looks like it enjoyed a few years of outsized premium multiple around Covid, which would have dragged its whole average even higher.

That said, should we get any sort of signs that they are on their way to returning to an earnings growth trajectory and sales bottom out, I think this could be another strong candidate as a turnaround play. Though it could likely be a few quarters before we start to get any idea if they are likely to be successful or not.

data.title: Allspring Common Stock Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly review

  • The Allspring Common Stock Fund underperformed the Russell 2500 Index during the period.
  • Stock selection in health care and consumer discretionary sectors and underweight allocation to energy detracted from relative performance.
  • Selection in financials benefit performance in the quarter.

Market review

U.S. equity markets declined during the first quarter of 2026, reflecting a challenging macroeconomic and policy environment. Elevated valuations, shifting expectations around the path of monetary policy, and renewed geopolitical tensions weighed on sentiment. Oil prices jumped to their highest levels in three years, while prices for soft commodities also increased. Meanwhile, gold and silver declined from their recent peaks, as rising yields and shifting rate expectations appeared to reduce the appeal of precious metals, highlighting how investors are reacting to changing inflation and policy trends. The S&P 500 Index fell 4.3% for the quarter, with most of the weakness occurring early amid inflation concerns, questions around the timing of Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cuts, and higher energy prices. While geopolitical tensions added to volatility by pressuring energy markets and inflation expectations, investors remained focused on earnings fundamentals rather than a broader economic disruption.

A late-quarter rebound helped stabilize markets but was not enough to offset earlier losses. Despite weaker headline results, underlying market conditions were more balanced than index performance suggested. Declines were largely concentrated among a narrow group of mega-cap stocks, particularly within growth-oriented segments sensitive to higher rates, while leadership shifted toward areas with more durable earnings and cash flows. Energy led the market, supported by higher commodity prices and supply-side disruptions, alongside strength in utilities, materials, and select industrials. Within information technology (IT), performance varied, as companies tied to artificial intelligence (AI)-related infrastructure held up better than software and other more rate-sensitive growth segments. While consensus earnings expectations for 2026 remained constructive, elevated valuations limited broader index gains.

Small-and mid-capitalization stocks were relatively more resilient. The Russell 2500 Index returned 2.04% for the quarter, modestly outperforming large-cap equities. More balanced sector exposure and reduced concentration helped support results, with value-oriented and higher-quality segments outperforming large-cap growth, though tighter financial conditions continued to challenge more leveraged companies. Investor preferences increasingly favored businesses with strong cash flows, solid balance sheets, and greater insulation from rising input costs.

Fund performance and attribution

Top Contributors to and Detractors from Quarter-end Fund Performance

CONTRIBUTORS

  • Littelfuse, Inc.
  • Regal Rexnord Corp.
  • Westlake Corp.
  • Kirby Corp.
  • Coterra Energy Inc.

DETRACTORS

  • Azenta, Inc.
  • Herc Holdings, Inc.
  • SanDisk Corp.
  • Planet Fitness, Inc. Class A
  • Revolve Group, Inc. Class A
The holdings identified do not represent all of the securities purchased or sold during the period shown and should not be construed as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. Information on calculation methodology and a list showing the overall contribution of each holding in the account for the period shown are available upon request.

The Allspring Common Stock Fund underperformed the Russell 2500 Index during the period. Performance was mainly driven by negative stock selection, with sector allocation also having a slight negative effect on overall results. Health care was the most significant detractor, led by weakness within life sciences tools and services, while consumer discretionary and IT also weighed on relative performance. Financials was the largest contributor, supported by strong stock selection within capital markets and select banking names.

The broader market backdrop was mixed during the period. Strength in cyclical areas such as energy and industrials reflected improving optimism around industrial activity and higher commodity prices, while smaller- and mid-cap growth-oriented sectors faced pressure from elevated interest rates, volatility in IT, and heightened scrutiny of earnings visibility. Within this environment, the portfolio's exposure to select financials provided relative support but was outweighed by declines in several individual holdings.

Stocks Contributing to Relative Performance Include:

Littelfuse, Inc. (LFUS), designs and manufactures circuit protection, sensing, and power control products serving electronics, automotive, and industrial end markets. LFUS delivered strong performance in the quarter as results and guidance exceeded expectations, supported by stronger bookings (B/B > 1 and bookings up >20% year over year) and solid backlog entering 2026, which drove improved operating leverage. Performance was led by the electronics segment, where growth tied to data center, grid, utilities, and renewable applications more than offset softness in residential HVAC, commercial vehicles, and certain areas of power semiconductors. Management highlighted increasing encouragement around early signs of industrial recovery and continued outgrowth in automotive while previewing portfolio actions ahead of a planned May analyst day, including deeper integration of recent acquisitions and a sharper focus on higher-value semiconductor applications. Data center and AI-related demand were also cited as areas of increasing strategic importance as power architectures grow more complex.

Regal Rexnord Corp. (RRX) engineers and manufactures factory automation sub-systems, industrial powertrain solutions, automation and mechanical power transmission components, electric motors and electronic controls, air moving products, and specialty electrical components and systems. RRX performed strongly in the first quarter, primarily on account of 1) a quarterly report that featured particularly strong orders owing to budding demand for RRX electrical products sold into the data center market, which drives a substantial revision to 2027 growth expectations, and 2) two >50 Institute for Supply Management Manufacturing Purchasing Manager's Index prints following three years of sub-50 conditions. We consider RRX to be an attractive security offering exposure to improving prospects for growth in U.S. manufacturing investment, with an expanding side opportunity for growing its small data center electrical business.

Westlake Corp. (WLK) is a global diversified chemical and building products company that engages in the manufacturing and supplying of PVC, caustic soda, polyethylene, and various building products. WLK outperformed in the first quarter due to the effects on global chemical markets from the conflict in Iran, which has had an extreme impact on the supply of global chemical products. Various competing global chemical producers have declared unforeseen circumstances or had their input costs materially inflate, while WLK's operations and costs have been largely unaffected, as its facilities are located in the U.S. and thus have access to unaffected U.S. natural gas supplies. The situation highlights WLK's core advantage pertaining to its place on the global cost curve and security of operations. WLK's separate building products company brings exposure to eventual improvements off of depressed levels in home improvement spending and construction activity.

Kirby Corp. (KEX) provides inland and coastal marine transportation services for bulk liquid products and distributes equipment and services to energy and industrial customers. KEX performed well in the quarter as marine transportation results improved through the period, with inland barge utilization recovering meaningfully by quarter-end and margins coming in better than expected. While pricing softened modestly during the quarter, management characterized the decline as temporary, citing favorable renewal activity and continued capacity discipline. KEX has also benefited from power generation needs from data centers in their engines segment and should benefit from any commodity production increases in the U.S. to offset shut-in capacity globally related to the Iran conflict.

Stocks Detracting from Relative Performance Include:

Azenta, Inc. (AZTA), provides biological and chemical sample management, genomics, and laboratory automation solutions to research and clinical customers, generating revenue through service contracts and consumables. AZTA underperformed the benchmark during the quarter as profitability weakened, with adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) margins pressured by underused lab capacity, Automated Stores remediation costs, and softer demand in portions of Sample Management Solutions. Organic revenue in Sample Management declined modestly, reflecting continued challenges in the Automated Stores and Cryo businesses, while earnings fell year over year despite modest topline growth. AZTA completed the acquisition of UK Biocentre, which is expected to dilute margins in fiscal 2026 but expands European biorepository capabilities and supports longer-term growth objectives.

Herc Holdings, Inc. (HRI), provides equipment rental solutions to construction, industrial, and infrastructure customers, generating revenue primarily from fleet rentals and related services. HRI performed poorly during the quarter as investor attention centered on elevated leverage and near-term execution risk despite solid fourth-quarter results and rental demand. Management spent the period focused on integrating the H&E Equipment Services acquisition, including branch conversions and sales territory optimization that added operational complexity early in the year. The company also implemented a new regional operating structure to support future scale and efficiency gains, though benefits have yet to be reflected in sentiment. Overall, the quarter highlighted steady underlying demand but ongoing integration and balance sheet considerations that weighed on shares.

Planet Fitness, Inc. (PLNT), operates a franchised and company-owned fitness center network, generating revenue from memberships, franchise royalties, and equipment sales. Shares fell after cautious full-year guidance signaled slower sales and earnings growth, despite strong quarterly performance. While revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and earnings exceeded expectations, management highlighted near-term headwinds, including weaker join trends early in the year, higher churn in January, and a more back-half-weighted club-opening schedule. Additional pressure came from lower expected equipment sales and incremental cost headwinds that tempered the outlook for fiscal 2026. The quarter reflected steady brand demand and continued unit growth, but investor sentiment shifted toward normalization after several years of outsized growth.

Revolve Group, Inc. (RVLV), operates an online fashion platform selling apparel, footwear, and beauty products to a global, digitally native customer base. RVLV had lackluster performance during the quarter as investor reaction centered on higher planned marketing spend and a more cautious margin outlook, despite solid revenue growth and improving customer engagement. Results showed accelerating demand, supported by broad-based category growth and increased active customer additions, but near-term profitability expectations were tempered as the company leaned into brand investment. Management also continued to expand its own brand portfolio and beauty offering, which contributed to growth but weighed modestly on average order values. Overall, the quarter reflected strong topline execution while near-term expense discipline remained the primary focus for investors.

Fund positioning and outlook

Markets are likely to remain sensitive to macroeconomic developments, including the path of Fed policy, inflation trends, and evolving geopolitical and trade dynamics. While expectations for eventual rate cuts remain supportive, uncertainty around timing and policy outcomes may sustain volatility, particularly within small-and mid-cap equities. Against this backdrop, we remain steadfast in our commitment to valuation discipline and broad-based diversification, ensuring portfolios are not overexposed to any one theme or factor. By focusing on durable businesses and maintaining a selective, nimble approach, we believe the strategy is well positioned to navigate near-term volatility and capture long-term opportunities as market leadership broadens and value regains prominence.

Benchmark Descriptions:

The Russell 2500™ Index measures the performance of the small-to mid-cap segment of the U.S. equity universe. It is a subset of the Russell 3000® Index and includes approximately 2,500 of the smallest securities based on a combination of their market capitalization and current index membership. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The S&P 500 Index consists of 500 stocks chosen for market size, liquidity, and industry group representation. It is a market-value-weighted index with each stock's weight in the index proportionate to its market value. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Institute of Supply Management (ISM) Manufacturing Purchasing Managers Index (PMI) is an index of the prevailing direction of economic trends in the United States manufacturing and service sectors. It consists of a diffusion index that summarizes whether market conditions, as viewed by purchasing managers, are expanding, staying the same, or contracting. The purpose of the PMI is to provide information about current and future business conditions to company decision makers, analysts, and investors. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Lipper averages are compiled by Lipper, Inc., an independent mutual fund research and rating service. Each Lipper average represents a universe of funds that are similar in investment objective. You cannot invest directly in a Lipper average.

The Morningstar Category average is the average return for the peer group based on the returns of each individual fund within the group. The total return of the Morningstar Category average does not include the effect of sales charges. You cannot invest directly in a Morningstar Category average.

Diversification does not ensure or guarantee better performance and cannot eliminate the risk of investment losses.

Attribution Analysis:

Performance attribution and sector returns are calculated using the Brinson-Fachler attribution model. As such, performance attribution calculations may differ from the fund's actual investment results. Common sources of attribution dispersion from official returns include: the use of end of day holdings and prices, the exclusion of fees, and the use of myriad pricing sources to accommodate different benchmark providers.

Definition of Terms:

Alpha measures the difference between a fund's actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Downside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of negative returns. During periods of negative benchmark returns, a downside capture ratio less than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and a downside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Information ratio measures the consistency of excess return (return in excess of a benchmark). This value is determined by taking the annualized excess return over a benchmark (style benchmark by default) and dividing it by the standard deviation of excess return.

R-squared is a measurement of how similar a fund's historical performance has been to that of the benchmark. The measure ranges from 0.0, which means that the fund's performance bears no relationship to the performance of the index, to 1.0, which means that the fund's performance was perfectly synchronized with the performance of the benchmark.

Sharpe ratio measures the potential reward offered by a mutual fund relative to its risk level. The ratio uses a fund's standard deviation and its excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. The higher the Sharpe ratio, the better the fund's historical risk-adjusted performance.

Standard deviation represents the degree to which an investment's performance has varied from its average performance over a particular time period.

Tracking error measures the extent to which a manager's performance mimics that of a benchmark. The value is the standard deviation of the difference between a fund's performance and a benchmark's performance.

Upside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of positive returns. During periods of positive benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Risks:

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Stock values fluctuate in response to the activities of individual companies and general market and economic conditions. Smaller company stocks tend to be more volatile and less liquid than those of larger companies. Consult the fund's prospectus for additional information on these and other risks.

The Morningstar Rating for funds, or star rating, is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar risk-adjusted return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The Morningstar Rating does not include any adjustment for sales loads. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36–59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60–119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods.

Across U.S.-domiciled mid-cap blend funds, the Common Stock Fund received 1 star among 365 funds, 1 star among 341 funds, and 1 star among 263 funds for the 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods, respectively. The Morningstar Rating is for the Institutional Class only; other classes may have different performance characteristics.

The Morningstar absolute ranking is based on the fund's total return rank relative to all funds that have the same category for the same time period. Morningstar rankings do not include the effect of sales charges. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.
